data.title: Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.) (DSM:NLCS) Posted Healthy Earnings But There Are Some Other Factors To Be Aware Of
data.description:
Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.)'s (DSM:NLCS) robust earnings report didn't manage to move the market for its stock. Our analysis suggests that shareholders have noticed something concerning in the numbers.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

The Power Of Non-Operating Revenue

Companies will classify their revenue streams as either operating revenue or other revenue. Where possible, we prefer rely on operating revenue to get a better understanding of how the business is functioning. However, we note that when non-operating revenue increases suddenly, it will sometimes generate an unsustainable boost to profit. Notably, Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.) had a significant increase in non-operating revenue over the last year. In fact, our data indicates that non-operating revenue increased from ر.ق33.3m to ر.ق40.9m. The high levels of non-operating revenue are problematic because if (and when) they do not repeat, then overall revenue (and profitability) of the firm will fall. Sometimes, you can get a better idea of the underlying earnings potential of a company by excluding unusual boosts to non-operating revenue.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.).

Our Take On Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.)'s Profit Performance

When considering the nature of Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.)'s earnings, we'd absolutely keep in mind that it saw an increase in non-operating revenue in the last year, which would in turn have boosted its profit, potentially in an unsustainable manner. Because of this, we think that it may be that Alijarah Holding (Q.P.S.C.)'s statutory profits are better than its underlying earnings power. Nonetheless, it's still worth noting that its earnings per share have grown at 8.6% over the last three years. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. Keep in mind, when it comes to analysing a stock it's worth noting the risks involved. Euronet Worldwide has announced a strategic acquisition of the Spanish fintech company PaynoPain, enhancing its omnichannel payment solutions and merchant services in Spain and Portugal. This move strengthens Euronet’s position in the European merchant acquiring landscape, leveraging PaynoPain’s digital-first payment technologies. The acquisition aligns with Euronet’s strategy to expand its global payments infrastructure and deliver comprehensive payment solutions that cater to the growing demand for e-commerce and omnichannel capabilities. The deal is subject to regulatory approvals and is expected to close in the third quarter of 2026.

In other trading, Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) was a notable mover up 8.1% and closing at $45.36, close to the 52-week high. The company reported a significant turnaround in its Q1 2026 earnings results, showing net income of $319 million, a stark contrast to the net loss a year ago, announced three days ago.

On a P/E of 5.9x, Q Technology is priced well below both its peers at 58.4x and the Hong Kong Electronic industry average of 12x, even after the recent share price strength to HK$8.49.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share, so it gives you a quick sense of how much you are paying for each unit of profit. For a hardware and modules business like Q Technology, this is often a core yardstick because earnings already reflect margins, cost control, and product mix across its main lines in camera modules, biometric modules, and automation equipment.

What stands out is that this low P/E sits alongside very strong recent earnings growth, higher net profit margins than last year, and a Return on Equity of 23.3%, which is described as high. At the same time, earnings are forecast to decline on average by 4.6% per year over the next 3 years, so the current discount could reflect expectations that the latest profit surge is hard to repeat. The estimated fair P/E of 8x also suggests a level that the market could move towards if pricing ever aligned more closely with that regression based fair ratio.

Compared with the peer average P/E of 58.4x and the broader Hong Kong Electronic industry on 12x, Q Technology's 5.9x multiple is described as good value relative to both. That combination of a lower P/E than the market at 12.6x and an estimated fair P/E of 8x makes the gap between current pricing and the fair ratio worth watching.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Reassess Palantir Technologies (PLTR) After Its 61% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering whether Palantir Technologies at US$146.39 still makes sense for your portfolio, or if expectations have already done the heavy lifting on the share price?
  • The stock has returned 10.6% over the last 7 days, while the 30 day and year to date returns are a 2.8% decline and a 12.8% decline, set against a 61.2% gain over the last year and a very large 3 year return.
  • Recent commentary has focused on Palantir's role in data analytics and software for governments and enterprises, along with ongoing debates about how durable demand for its platforms could be. This mix of optimism and caution has kept attention on whether the current share price leaves much room for error.
  • Against that backdrop, Palantir holds a valuation score of 0 out of 6. The next sections will compare different valuation approaches to that score and then finish with a broader way to think about what the market might be pricing in.

Palantir Technologies scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Palantir Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return.

For Palantir Technologies, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on its last twelve months free cash flow of about $2.1b. Analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates point to projected free cash flow of $13.3b in 2030, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2027 modeled at $4.0b and $5.6b respectively. Beyond the analyst horizon, Simply Wall St extends those projections through 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

When all those future cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $122.51 per share. Compared to the current share price of $146.39, this implies the stock is roughly 19.5% overvalued based on these inputs and assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Palantir Technologies may be overvalued by 19.5%. Discover 60 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Palantir Technologies.

Approach 2: Palantir Technologies Price vs Book

Price based multiples such as P/B are often used for profitable companies because they tie the share price to an underlying economic anchor, in this case the accounting value of net assets. Investors usually accept a higher multiple when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower multiple when growth expectations are modest or risks feel higher.

Palantir Technologies currently trades on a P/B of 47.39x. That sits well above the Software industry average P/B of 2.82x and also above the peer group average of 23.07x. On simple comparisons, the stock carries a much richer valuation than many industry peers.

Simply Wall St also uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio”, which estimates what a reasonable P/B could be for Palantir after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, market cap, risk profile and industry. This tailored yardstick can be more helpful than a basic peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for the company’s specific characteristics rather than treating all software firms as similar. In this case, Palantir’s current P/B of 47.39x sits materially above the Fair Ratio, which points to a stretched valuation on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

This is where Narratives come in, a simple way for you to tie your view of Palantir Technologies to concrete numbers like future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value estimate, then compare that to today’s price.

A Narrative on Simply Wall St is your story for the company, written as assumptions, which the platform turns into a financial forecast and a fair value. Because it sits on the Community page used by millions of investors, you can see how your view lines up with others.

These Narratives are not static. They refresh when new information such as earnings or news is added, so your fair value and the gap between that value and the current share price can move as the facts change, helping you decide whether Palantir Technologies looks expensive or cheap relative to your own expectations.

For example, one Palantir Technologies Narrative on the platform assumes a fair value around US$19.79 while another sits near US$5,967.47. This shows how different stories about the same business can lead to very different conclusions and why choosing the Narrative that fits your view matters.
